title: Linen rag pulp, making and beating
subtitle: Preparing linen fibre from old cloth for high-quality hand papermaking
excerpt: Linen rag produces some of the finest Western hand papermaking pulp: long fibre, high strength, and a characteristic laid-line definition when pulled on a wove or laid mould. The preparation takes longer than cotton linter but the resulting sheets are noticeably stronger and more permanent.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), public domain. Documents the use of linen rags as the primary raw material of Western hand papermaking from its introduction to Europe. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/oldpapermaking00hunt. Additional reference: general public-domain papermaking literature from the same period.

glossaryTerms.definition: Paper pulp prepared from washed linen cloth cut into small pieces and beaten to separate the long cellulose fibres. Produces a strong, permanent paper with excellent writing and printing qualities.
A measure of how readily a pulp drains water. A free pulp drains quickly through the mould mesh; a slow pulp holds water longer, allowing more fibre to settle before the sheet forms.

content.text: as their main raw material from the thirteenth century until the mid-nineteenth century, when wood-pulp made cotton and chemical pulp practical at scale. Linen rags produce long, strong fibres. The sheets hold writing inks and printing pigments well and resist the yellowing that affects wood-pulp papers over time.

attrs.title: Safety: blender use near water
attrs.body: Pulping paper is electrical work near water. Keep the blender unplugged when loading fibre or rinsing out. Drain the vat before reaching in with both hands. Treat the blender as you would any workshop machine near a water source.

items.name: Linen rags (slug: linen-rag-pulp)
items.description: Old linen tea towels, shirt fabric, or plain linen cloth, washed clean. Cut into 20-30 mm squares. Remove any synthetic threads, buttons, or non-linen trims.

items.name: Water
items.description: The cleaner the better. Hard water can leave mineral deposits; if your water is very hard, use filtered or collected rainwater for a cleaner sheet.

items.name: Papermaking blender (slug: papermaking-blender)
items.description: A dedicated blender kept separate from food use. A standard kitchen blender works but the linen is harder on the blades than cotton.

items.name: Papermaking vat (slug: papermaking-vat)
items.description: Wide and low-sided, larger than the mould.

items.name: Papermaking sponge (slug: papermaking-sponge)
items.description: For removing excess water during couching.

content.text: Traditional papermakers retted their rags: soaked them in water for days or weeks to begin breaking down the fibre structure. At home, soaking the cut linen pieces in warm water for 12 to 24 hours softens the fibres enough for blending. Change the water after the first soaking to remove any residual sizing or dye.
The fibres do not need cooking or chemical treatment unless the cloth is heavily sized. Plain woven linen that has been washed many times and is soft to the touch blends well without pretreatment.

Load the blender to one-third full with water. Add a handful of soaked linen pieces. Blend on a high setting for 60 seconds. The mixture should look milky and fine. Longer blending gives a finer, slower-draining pulp; shorter blending leaves longer fibres that make a rougher sheet. For a first trial, 60 seconds is a good starting point.

Pour the blended pulp into the vat and add water to reach a working depth of 200-250 mm. Stir thoroughly before pulling each sheet: linen fibres settle faster than cotton linter and the vat needs stirring between every pull.

Linen pulp after 60 seconds of blending has a lower freeness than cotton linter: it drains more slowly. A slower pulp allows the fibres to settle more evenly before the sheet begins to form, which generally produces a more uniform sheet. If the sheet is forming very unevenly (with thick and thin patches), blend for longer to reduce fibre length and improve freeness.
